AuthenticationAssuranceLevel: AAL 2 - Aadhaar OTP authentication
Description: Digital ID is used for electronic verification of income tax returns, enabling instant validation without physical document submission. Operational since 2015, the Income Tax Department in collaboration with UIDAI allows taxpayers to e-verify their filed Income Tax Returns (ITRs) using Aadhaar-based One-Time Password (OTP) authentication. After filing an ITR through the Income Tax e-filing portal, taxpayers must verify their return within 30 days for it to be considered valid. Aadhaar-based e-verification has become the most popular verification method, allowing taxpayers to instantly verify their returns by receiving an OTP on their Aadhaar-registered mobile number and entering it on the e-filing portal. Over 6.21 crore (62.1 million) ITRs have been e-verified, of which more than 5.81 crore (58.1 million) used Aadhaar-based OTP, representing 93.56% of all e-verified returns. This method has largely replaced older verification processes including sending physically signed ITR-V (verification form) by post to the Centralized Processing Center (CPC) in Bengaluru, net banking verification, bank ATM verification, and Demat account verification. The Aadhaar-based e-verification system has revolutionized tax compliance in India by providing instant return verification without requiring taxpayers to print, sign, and mail physical documents, reducing ITR processing time from weeks to days, eliminating verification delays that previously led to delayed refunds, ensuring secure authentication through government-verified biometric identity, lowering compliance costs for taxpayers and administrative costs for the Income Tax Department, and supporting India's digital tax administration transformation under the Digital India initiative.

Title: Jeevan Pramaan (Digital Life Certificate)
KeyTerms: Pension verification, Biometric authentication, Life certificate, Pensioner services
AuthenticationAssuranceLevel: AAL 2 - Fingerprint or iris biometric authentication
Description: Digital ID is used for biometric authentication to generate digital life certificates for pensioners, eliminating the need for physical verification. Jeevan Pramaan, launched in November 2014 by the Ministry of Personnel, Public Grievances and Pensions in collaboration with UIDAI and banks, enables pensioners to submit their annual life certificate digitally using Aadhaar-based biometric authentication instead of appearing physically before pension disbursing authorities. Pensioners are required to submit a life certificate annually, typically in November, to confirm they are alive and eligible to continue receiving pension payments. Through Jeevan Pramaan, pensioners can authenticate their identity using fingerprint or iris scan at designated service centers, Common Service Centers (CSCs), or through the Jeevan Pramaan mobile app using registered biometric devices. The system instantly generates a Digital Life Certificate (DLC) which is directly transmitted to the Pension Disbursing Agency (bank or government department), eliminating paperwork and manual submission. Between November 1-11, 2024 alone, 48.10 lakh (4.81 million) Digital Life Certificates were generated, demonstrating widespread adoption of this digital service among India's elderly population. The Jeevan Pramaan system has transformed pension administration by providing convenience to senior citizens who previously had to travel to pension offices, reducing administrative burden on pension disbursing agencies, preventing delays in pension payments due to late certificate submission, enabling pensioners living abroad to submit certificates through designated centers, providing real-time verification and reducing processing time from weeks to minutes, and supporting dignified aging by reducing bureaucratic hassles for elderly citizens.

Title: APAAR (Automated Permanent Academic Account Registry) Student ID
KeyTerms: Education, Digital education, Academic records, Student identity, DigiLocker, Academic Bank of Credits
AuthenticationAssuranceLevel: Not available
Description: Digital ID is used to create a lifelong unique student identifier linked to Aadhaar that tracks academic progress from primary to higher education. The Automated Permanent Academic Account Registry (APAAR), also known as the "One Nation One Student ID," was introduced by the Ministry of Education as part of the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020 to create a unified digital education ecosystem. Each student receives a unique 12-digit APAAR ID (also called ABC ID when used in higher education) that is linked to their Aadhaar number, serving as a permanent academic identifier throughout their educational journey from kindergarten through post-graduation and beyond. The APAAR ID serves multiple functions: it acts as a gateway to DigiLocker, an online repository where students can securely store and access essential documents such as mark sheets, certificates, degrees, and exam results; it links to the Academic Bank of Credits (ABC), which maintains a digital ledger of all academic credits earned by students across institutions, enabling credit transfer and recognition of prior learning; and it facilitates seamless student mobility across educational institutions within India. As of early 2025, over 2 crore (20 million) students have been registered under the APAAR ID system nationwide, with pilot implementations underway across various states.

Title: Aadhaar verification in Staff Selection Commission (SSC) examinations
KeyTerms: Examination, Education, Candidate verification
AuthenticationAssuranceLevel: AAL 2 - Multi-modal Aadhaar authentication (OTP, face, fingerprint, iris)
Description: Digital ID is used for multi-stage candidate verification throughout the Staff Selection Commission (SSC) examination lifecycle to prevent impersonation and examination fraud. Launched in May 2025 by the Staff Selection Commission in collaboration with UIDAI, the voluntary Aadhaar-based authentication system employs multiple verification touchpoints: Aadhaar OTP-based verification during one-time candidate registration on the SSC portal, Aadhaar face authentication while submitting examination application forms, biometric verification (fingerprint and iris scan) at the examination center on the test day, and Aadhaar authentication during medical examination and final document verification for selected candidates. The SSC conducts recruitment examinations for various Group B and Group C posts in ministries, departments, and organizations of the Government of India, attracting millions of candidates annually. Effective May 2025, candidates appearing for SSC exams including Combined Graduate Level (CGL), Combined Higher Secondary Level (CHSL), Junior Engineer (JE), and other examinations can voluntarily opt for Aadhaar verification during online registration, application submission, and at examination centers. The multi-modal Aadhaar authentication system offers several benefits including smoother candidate check-in processes with fewer manual document verifications, reduced reporting time at examination centers, prevention of proxy test-takers through biometric matching, elimination of forged admit cards and identity documents, real-time authentication against the UIDAI database, and enhanced integrity of the competitive examination process. The system supports SSC's mandate to conduct fair, transparent, and fraud-free examinations for recruitment to central government positions.

Title: Insurance policy issuance and claims verification
KeyTerms: Insurance, eKYC, Claims processing, Fraud prevention
AuthenticationAssuranceLevel: AAL 2 - Aadhaar-based biometric and OTP authentication
Description: Digital ID is used for customer identity verification during insurance policy issuance and for authenticating claimants during claims processing. Operational since 2017, Aadhaar-based e-KYC enables insurance companies to verify policyholders' identities during policy purchase, reducing the need for physical documentation and enabling paperless policy issuance for life insurance, health insurance, general insurance, and pension products. The Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority of India (IRDAI), in collaboration with UIDAI and insurance companies, has integrated Aadhaar authentication into the insurance lifecycle to improve customer onboarding, prevent fraudulent policies, and streamline claims settlement. In 2024, IRDAI issued instructions mandating all insurers to link new and existing insurance policies with Aadhaar numbers to prevent fraudulent claims and ensure accurate identification of policyholders and beneficiaries.

Title: Know Your Customer (KYC) verification for financial institutions
KeyTerms: eKYC, Customer onboarding, AML/CFT compliance, Financial regulation
AuthenticationAssuranceLevel: AAL 2 - Aadhaar-based biometric and OTP authentication
Description: Digital ID is used to complete Know Your Customer (KYC) verification during customer onboarding across banks, Non-Banking Financial Companies (NBFCs) including Microfinance Institutions (MFIs), payment service providers, and FinTech companies. Operational since 2016, Aadhaar-based e-KYC allows regulated financial entities to verify customer identities electronically through multiple methods: online e-KYC using biometric or OTP authentication, and offline KYC using digitally signed XML files or QR codes from the mAadhaar app, which contain masked Aadhaar numbers and demographic details. This system, regulated by the Reserve Bank of India (RBI) and UIDAI, enables instant, paperless customer verification while maintaining compliance with RBI's KYC norms and Anti-Money Laundering/Combating the Financing of Terrorism (AML/CFT) regulations. The offline KYC method, introduced to address privacy concerns following the 2018 Supreme Court judgment, allows customers to share their verified identity information without exposing their 12-digit Aadhaar number to service providers. In January 2025 alone, more than 43 crore (430 million) e-KYC transactions were carried out, with cumulative Aadhaar e-KYC transactions exceeding 2,268 crore (22.68 billion) by the end of January 2025. In March 2025, 39.50 million successful e-KYC transactions were recorded through the AePS platform alone.

Title: Aadhaar for bank account opening
KeyTerms: Financial inclusion, eKYC, Jan Dhan Yojana, Banking access
AuthenticationAssuranceLevel: AAL 2 - Aadhaar-based biometric and OTP authentication
Description: Digital ID is used to perform electronic Know Your Customer (e-KYC) verification for opening bank accounts, reducing the need for physical documentation and enabling instant account activation. Aadhaar-based e-KYC, conducted through biometric authentication and OTP verification against the UIDAI database, allows banks to verify customer identity instantly and open accounts on the spot. This process has been instrumental in the success of the Pradhan Mantri Jan Dhan Yojana (PMJDY), launched in 2014, which aimed to achieve universal financial inclusion by providing every household with at least one basic banking account. As of May 2025, 55.39 crore (553.9 million) Jan Dhan accounts have been opened using Aadhaar as the primary identity verification instrument. Aadhaar-based account opening is overseen by the Reserve Bank of India (RBI) and implemented by all scheduled commercial banks, regional rural banks, and cooperative banks across India.

Title: Aadhaar enabled Payment System (AePS)
KeyTerms: Financial inclusion, Micro-ATM
AuthenticationAssuranceLevel: AAL 2 - Fingerprint biometric authentication
Description:
Digital ID can be used for biometric authentication to enable banking transactions through micro-ATMs operated by Business Correspondents (BCs) in remote and underserved areas. 


The Aadhaar enabled Payment System (AePS), launched by the National Payments Corporation of India (NPCI) in collaboration with UIDAI and banks, allows customers to perform basic banking services including cash withdrawal, cash deposit, balance inquiry, mini statements, and Aadhaar-to-Aadhaar fund transfers using only their Aadhaar number and biometric authentication (fingerprint) without requiring a debit card, PIN, or smartphone. AePS operates through a network of Business Correspondents equipped with biometric devices and micro-ATMs, bringing banking services to India's rural and semi-urban populations where traditional bank branches and ATMs are scarce. The system provides bank interoperability, allowing customers to access their accounts from any bank through any BC point. 


In March 2025, AePS recorded 570.72 million transactions, with monthly transaction volumes consistently ranging between 462 million and 618 million transactions from April 2024 to March 2025, demonstrating sustained adoption.

Title: Customer verification for mobile SIM purchase
KeyTerms: Telecom, eKYC, SIM registration
AuthenticationAssuranceLevel: AAL 2 - Aadhaar-based biometric authentication
Description: Digital ID is used for instant customer verification during mobile SIM card purchase and activation through Aadhaar-based electronic Know Your Customer (e-KYC) processes. Launched in 2016 by the Department of Telecommunications (DoT) in collaboration with UIDAI and telecom operators, Aadhaar-based e-KYC enabled paperless, instant SIM activation by authenticating customers' identities through biometric verification (fingerprint or iris scan) against the Aadhaar database. This process eliminated the need for physical document submission and manual verification, reducing SIM activation time from days to minutes. The system was widely adopted by major telecom operators including Airtel, Vodafone Idea, Reliance Jio, and BSNL, becoming the dominant method for new mobile connections across India. However, following the Supreme Court's September 2018 judgment in the Justice K.S. Puttaswamy case, which restricted mandatory use of Aadhaar to services backed by law, Aadhaar-based e-KYC for SIM cards became voluntary rather than mandatory. Telecom operators must now also accept alternative identity documents such as passport, voter ID, driving license, or PAN card for customer verification.

Title: Student scholarship disbursement
KeyTerms: Education, Education financing, Student welfare, eKYC, DBT
AuthenticationAssuranceLevel: AAL 1 - Aadhaar-based demographic authentication
Description: Digital ID is used to authenticate student identities during scholarship applications and enable direct transfer of scholarship funds into Aadhaar-linked bank accounts. The National Scholarship Portal (NSP), launched in 2015 by the Ministry of Education in collaboration with State Education Departments, serves as a unified platform for students to apply for various scholarships offered by central and state governments. Aadhaar authentication ensures that only eligible students receive benefits and prevents duplicate or fraudulent claims. Students must provide their Aadhaar number during online registration on the NSP portal, which verifies their identity against the UIDAI (Unique Identification Authority of India) database. Once verified and approved, scholarship amounts are transferred directly to students' Aadhaar-seeded bank accounts through Direct Benefit Transfer (DBT), eliminating intermediaries and ensuring timely receipt of funds. The NSP covers multiple scholarship schemes including pre-matric, post-matric, merit-cum-means, and minority scholarships. As an example, in Nagaland for the academic year 2024-2025, 41,793 beneficiaries received post-matric scholarships totaling ₹56.38 crore (approximately USD 7 million), with all payments processed through Aadhaar-based authentication on the NSP. The National Scholarship Portal has received 15.88 lakh (1.58 million) Aadhaar-based applications.

Title: Pradhan Mantri Ujjwala Yojana (PMUY) - LPG connection subsidy
KeyTerms: Clean energy access, Energy subsidy, Women empowerment, eKYC
AuthenticationAssuranceLevel: AAL 2 - Aadhaar-based biometric authentication
Description: Digital ID is used to verify eligibility and authenticate beneficiaries for subsidized LPG (liquefied petroleum gas) connections under India's clean cooking fuel program. Launched in 2016 by the Ministry of Petroleum and Natural Gas, Pradhan Mantri Ujjwala Yojana (PMUY) aims to provide free or subsidized LPG connections to women from Below Poverty Line (BPL) households, replacing traditional biomass cooking fuels (firewood, coal, dung cakes) with clean cooking gas to improve health outcomes and reduce indoor air pollution. Aadhaar serves as the primary identity document for applicants seeking LPG connections, with applicants required to provide their Aadhaar cards to verify eligibility as members of poor households. The scheme leverages Aadhaar-based authentication to prevent duplicate connections, ensure subsidies reach intended beneficiaries, and enable Direct Benefit Transfer (DBT) of cooking gas subsidies directly into beneficiaries' Aadhaar-linked bank accounts. As of 2024, over 55% of PMUY beneficiaries have completed Biometric Aadhaar authentication. Significant biometric authentication drives were conducted during the Viksit Bharat Sankalp Yatra (Developed India Resolve Journey) camps, where more than 35 lakh (3.5 million) PMUY beneficiaries successfully completed Aadhaar authentication.  The Aadhaar integration has strengthened targeting accuracy, eliminated ghost beneficiaries, enabled efficient subsidy delivery, and supported India's energy transition and women's health objectives.

Title: Ayushman Bharat (PM-JAY) - Health insurance enrollment and service delivery
KeyTerms: Health insurance, Universal health coverage, Cashless treatment, eKYC
AuthenticationAssuranceLevel: AAL 2 - Aadhaar-based biometric authentication
Description: Digital ID is used to authenticate beneficiaries during enrollment in the health insurance scheme and to verify identity when accessing cashless medical treatment at empaneled hospitals. Ayushman Bharat Pradhan Mantri Jan Arogya Yojana (PM-JAY), launched in September 2018 by the National Health Authority (NHA) under the Ministry of Health & Family Welfare, provides health coverage of up to ₹5 lakh (approximately USD 6,000) per family per year for secondary and tertiary hospitalization to the bottom 40% of India's population. Aadhaar is used to authenticate beneficiaries during the creation of Ayushman cards, which serve as the primary health insurance identity document. Beneficiaries are encouraged to link their Ayushman cards with Aadhaar to ensure seamless identity verification and streamline the process of availing cashless medical services at over 27,000 empaneled public and private hospitals across India. As of March 2025, more than 36.9 crore (369 million) Ayushman cards have been created under the scheme. During hospital visits, beneficiaries present their Ayushman card and undergo Aadhaar-based authentication to verify eligibility before receiving treatment.

Title: Public Distribution System (PDS) - Aadhaar-based ration distribution
KeyTerms: Food security, Biometric authentication, Point-of-sale, eKYC
AuthenticationAssuranceLevel: AAL 2 - Fingerprint/iris biometric authentication at point of delivery
Description: Digital ID is used for real-time biometric authentication of beneficiaries at Fair Price Shops (FPS) during subsidized food grain distribution. India's Public Distribution System, one of the world's largest food security programs, was transformed beginning in 2015 through Aadhaar integration and installation of electronic Point-of-Sale (ePoS) devices at FPS outlets. Beneficiaries need to authenticate their identity using a fingerprint or iris scan at the point of transaction before receiving their entitled rations of rice, wheat, and other essential commodities. As of the July 2025, 20.11 crore (201 million) out of 20.21 crore ration cards have been seeded with Aadhaar, covering 77.43 crore (774 million) beneficiaries out of a total 80.66 crore (806 million) PDS beneficiaries. As of May 2023, approximately 97% of monthly PDS transactions nationwide are conducted through biometric authentication using ePoS devices, ensuring transparent and accountable distribution. The Aadhaar-based PDS system has dramatically reduced diversion of subsidized grains to black markets, eliminated ghost beneficiaries, enabled portability allowing beneficiaries to collect rations from any FPS across India (One Nation One Ration Card scheme), provided real-time transaction monitoring for government oversight, ensured intended beneficiaries receive their full entitlements, and generated significant fiscal savings by plugging leakages estimated at over ₹14,000 crore annually.

Title: Direct Benefit Transfer (DBT) - Multi-sector cash transfers
KeyTerms: Social protection, Financial inclusion, JAM Trinity, eKYC
AuthenticationAssuranceLevel: AAL 1 - Aadhaar-based demographic authentication
Description: Digital ID is used to authenticate beneficiaries and enable direct transfer of subsidies, welfare payments, and entitlements into Aadhaar-linked bank accounts. India's Direct Benefit Transfer (DBT) system, launched in 2013, leverages the JAM Trinity (Jan Dhan bank accounts, Aadhaar, and Mobile connectivity) to deliver benefits directly to citizens, eliminating intermediaries and reducing leakages. Aadhaar serves as the foundational identity for beneficiary authentication, bank account seeding, and payment reconciliation across multiple welfare schemes. The DBT framework has expanded from 28 central schemes in 2013-14 to over 321 schemes in 2025-26, covering sectors including rural employment (MGNREGS with 99.49% Aadhaar seeding of 13.55 crore active workers as of March 2025), farmer income support (PM-KISAN with over 11 crore enrolled farmers), maternity benefits (PMMVY providing ₹5,000 to pregnant and lactating mothers with Aadhaar-based verification and SMS status tracking), pensions, scholarships, and numerous state and central welfare programs. As of May 23, 2025, cumulative fund transfers through DBT reached ₹43,82,220 crore (approximately USD 527 billion), with cumulative savings of ₹3.48 lakh crore (approximately USD 42 billion) achieved by plugging leakages in welfare delivery. The Aadhaar-enabled DBT system has transformed India's social protection landscape by ensuring benefits reach intended recipients promptly, reducing corruption, improving financial inclusion by incentivizing bank account opening, enabling real-time payment tracking, and providing government with accurate beneficiary data for evidence-based policy making.
